title: Ukraine is under attack—but defiant
gist: Reporting from Kyiv, Jen Stout finds cautious optimism: Ukraine's superior drone and domestic-missile production is reversing Russian battlefield gains and upending great-power warfare, even as intensifying Russian strikes on Kyiv, Patriot shortages, manpower losses and souring Polish relations underline the war's fragility.

insights.claim: Ukraine can now launch drone strikes up to 200km away, wiping out Russian air defences and rear supply lines, which a K-2 brigade operator says has made it 'harder and harder' for Russia to sustain frontline logistics and advance.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Long-range cheap drones are structurally degrading Russian offensive capacity—key to the war's trajectory.

insights.claim: The FPV-drone 'kill zone' now stretches up to 30km either side of the frontline, making military resupply and casualty evacuation extremely difficult for both sides.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Quantifies how drone saturation is reshaping the physical logic of the battlefield.
insights.locator: Of course, Ukrainians face a drone-saturated

insights.claim: Ukraine is 'far superior' in ground drones—wheeled robots delivering supplies and evacuating wounded—and pulled off a robot-only assault in February 2026 using RPG-mounted platforms to clear a Russian position.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Ground-robotics leadership reduces Ukraine's reliance on vulnerable trucks and manpower.
insights.locator: So we’re not as reliant on trucks

insights.claim: Ukraine's ability to produce cheaper means of battlefield dominance—drones, interceptors, cheap missiles—is turning conventional notions of war and great powers upside down, letting the weak resist and exposing big powers' fragility.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: A structural thesis on cheap-mass warfare that reshapes global defence procurement priorities.

insights.claim: Gulf states are lining up to buy Ukrainian drone technology and expertise, with Ukraine now a world leader in this type of asymmetric warfare.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Ukraine is emerging as a defence-tech exporter—an arms-market shift to watch.

insights.claim: More than 50 per cent of the weapons Ukraine uses are now domestically produced, according to President Zelensky.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Rising self-sufficiency reduces Ukraine's exposure to fluctuating Western aid.
insights.locator: All the while, Ukraine was ramping up

insights.claim: Ukrainian company Fire Point makes Flamingo cruise missiles with a 3,000km range and 1,150kg payload, used extensively to strike military and industrial infrastructure deep inside Russia.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Indigenous long-range strike capability removes Western permission constraints on deep strikes.

insights.claim: Ukraine's defence industry claims production capacity 55 times bigger than in 2022 and aims to produce $60bn worth of weapons this year, and now makes its own missiles without asking anyone's permission to use them.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: A rapidly scaling wartime defence-industrial base with export and geopolitical implications.

insights.claim: Defence analyst Konrad Muzyka warns that many of Ukraine's most advanced drones depend on cooperation with American companies, and removing western investment, technology and software would leave Ukraine 'in a very tough spot'.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Tempers the self-sufficiency narrative—US tech dependence remains a critical single point of failure.

insights.claim: Ukraine remains heavily reliant on foreign aid, largely European money such as a recently approved €90bn loan funding soldier pay increases, amid an estimated 200,000 troops going AWOL.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Fiscal and manpower fragility underpins continued European funding demand.
insights.locator: The country is also still heavily reliant

insights.claim: Ukraine needs scarce Patriot missiles to intercept the weapons hitting Kyiv, and they are in short supply due to the war in Iran and dwindling US deliveries that predated it.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Air-defence interceptor scarcity is a direct vulnerability tied to competing US commitments.
insights.locator: Despite Ukraine’s innovation with drones and robots

insights.claim: In March and April 2026 Moscow lost control of around 120 square kilometres according to the Institute for the Study of War, reversing the trend of incremental Russian gains.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
entities: Institute for the Study of War
insights.so_what: A measurable shift in territorial momentum toward Ukraine after years of Russian creep.
insights.locator: But early this year, that trend slowed

insights.claim: On 24 May 2026 Russia broke its pattern by striking Kyiv's historic administrative centre—damaging the foreign ministry, cabinet building, an opera house and the Chornobyl museum—with Lavrov then threatening 'decision-making centres'.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Escalation into Kyiv's government core raises the war's political and diplomatic stakes.
insights.locator: As a rule, the administrative and historic centre

insights.claim: A subsequent overnight attack used 656 drones and 73 missiles, killed 16 people in Dnipro including children, and drove a record 41,000 people to shelter in the Kyiv metro.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Illustrates the scale of Russia's sustained aerial bombardment of Ukrainian cities.
insights.locator: It was Monday night when it finally started

insights.claim: Ukrainian thinkers see the 'Euro-Atlantic camp collapsing' and security guarantees losing meaning, so Ukrainian diplomacy is building new alliances 'where everyone is equal and small' rather than Cold War-style blocs.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Signals a shift toward multipolar, small-state alliance-building as US guarantees weaken.

insights.claim: As the US moves away from being Europe's security provider, defence analyst Konrad Muzyka argues indigenous capability is now urgent because 'if you do not have indigenous capability, then you will be servant to other people's interests'.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Reinforces the structural driver behind rising European defence-industrial investment.
insights.locator: As the US moves away from its role

insights.claim: The share of Ukrainians willing to endure the war 'as long as necessary' fell from over 70% in late 2024 to 54% in March 2026 and 48% in April 2026.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Declining war-endurance sentiment is a leading indicator for political pressure toward a settlement.
insights.locator: Until late 2024, more than 70 per cent

insights.claim: Despite falling endurance, a majority of Ukrainians still reject harsh 'peace' terms such as ceding more of Donbas, viewing a bad deal as pragmatically likely to lead to more war.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Domestic red lines constrain any negotiated settlement involving territorial concessions.

insights.claim: Polish-Ukrainian relations have soured—President Nawrocki stripped Zelensky of Poland's highest state honour over a military unit naming dispute, and far fewer Poles and Romanians now back aid to Ukraine than in 2022.
insights.speaker: Jen Stout
insights.so_what: Eroding regional support signals cracks in the coalition sustaining Ukraine.
insights.locator: All has not been rosy in the world of Ukrainian diplomacy

top_entities: Institute for the Study of War
